summary: McKenzie reports on his participation in a Bits Du Jour promotional deal, where Bingo Card Creator was offered at a 40% discount ($14.95 instead of $24.95). He praises the promotional copy written by Ellen, noting how she expanded his target market beyond teachers to parents, daycare providers, and corporate event coordinators. He reflects on the economics of time-limited promotions and smart market segmentation, noting that some use cases become viable at a lower price point. He expresses surprise at an early sale at 2 AM and anticipates the promotion will outperform his conservative expectation of two sales.
key_points: Time-limited discount promotions can expand your addressable market by making the product viable for use cases that don't justify the full price

Letting someone else write your promotional copy can reveal market segments you hadn't considered — Ellen identified parents, daycare providers, and corporate coordinators as audiences beyond teachers
Small experiments with capped downside (30 minutes of effort) are always worth trying, even if expectations are low
Customer purchase timing patterns reveal behavioral insights — teachers buy during lunch, after school, or after putting kids to bed
The promotional copy successfully reframes a niche educational tool as broadly useful through concrete, relatable scenarios
money_quotes: I'm always willing to take total losses on 30 minute experiments.

She originally started with what I had on my website as a base, then made a slight addition at my suggestion, and then went on to 'embrace and extend' my market to places where I hadn't even considered it would be useful.
It might not have been useful at $24.95, but was swingable at $14.95 — yay for smart market segmentation.

Teachers generally buy on their lunchbreak, shortly after school ends when planning lessons for the next day, or between the hours of 9 and 11 pm at night which I think is shorthand for 'after I have put the kids to sleep'.
Who orders Bingo Card Creator at 2 AM in the morning local time? Apparently, someone who saw the 40% discount and just said 'I gotta have it!'
key_insight: Low-risk promotional experiments can reveal untapped market segments, especially when a lower price point makes the product viable for use cases that wouldn't justify the full price.
